metadata.title: 3 On-Brand AI Images That Look Like a Set
metadata.playlist: Ship It — Build Your Brand's Live Presence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 8 brief — Building Your Live Website, Resumes & LinkedIn Header

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 5. Anyone can generate one good image. The grade is in three that look like siblings — same family, not three random stock photos. The secret is one prompt skeleton with three subject swaps. Lock the style tag, the palette words, and the lighting. Swap only the subject. That's the whole method.

props.segment: 3 On-Brand AI Images That Look Like a Set
props.command: One skeleton prompt. Three subject swaps. Three images that read as a set.
output: Style tag · palette words · lighting/medium — locked.

beats.act: what visual consistency means and how to engineer it
beats.narration_text: Visual consistency means a viewer can look at your three images side by side and believe they came from the same shoot, the same studio, or the same illustrator. You engineer this by locking exactly three variables in your prompt. First, the style tag — for example, flat illustration, editorial photography, or watercolor. That word or phrase controls the entire visual grammar. Second, the palette words — for example, terracotta and cream palette, or monochrome with a single amber accent. Color is the fastest signal the eye reads, and if it drifts across images, the set looks accidental. Third, the lighting or medium — for example, soft natural light from the left, or clean white studio background. Once those three are locked, you change only the subject. The subject is what each image is about. Everything else stays identical. That is the engineering, not the art — it's a constraint system.

artifactLines: 1. Style tag — controls the visual grammar: 'flat illustration', 'editorial photography', 'watercolor'.
2. Palette words — locks color across all three: 'terracotta and cream', 'monochrome with amber accent'.
3. Lighting or medium — locks the environment: 'soft natural light from the left', 'clean white studio background'.
Subject — the only variable that changes. Everything else is frozen.
Rule: if the viewer can't tell the three came from the same source, the skeleton is broken.

beats.narration_text: Here is the prompt skeleton. Everything marked LOCK never changes across the three images. Everything marked SWAP gets replaced once per image. The format is: your style tag — LOCK — comma, your palette words — LOCK — comma, your lighting or medium — LOCK — comma, representing your subject — SWAP THIS ONCE PER IMAGE. For image one, the subject is your expertise area: what you are specifically known for or what skill you lead with. For image two, the subject is your industry or field: the broader world your work lives in. For image three, the subject is your core value or offering: the outcome you create for clients, students, or employers. You paste this skeleton three times — changing only the subject line each time — and submit all three to the same image tool in the same session. That is the whole prompt protocol.

props.command: [STYLE_TAG — LOCK], [PALETTE_WORDS — LOCK], [LIGHTING/MEDIUM — LOCK], representing [SUBJECT — SWAP THIS ONCE PER IMAGE]. IMAGE 1 SUBJECT: my expertise area ([EXPERTISE]). IMAGE 2 SUBJECT: my industry or field ([INDUSTRY]). IMAGE 3 SUBJECT: my core value or offering ([VALUE]).
output: LOCK: style tag, palette words, lighting/medium — identical in all three prompts.
SWAP: subject line only — one swap per image.
Same tool, same session — consistency is a constraint, not a style choice.

beats.narration_text: Five tools will handle this. Midjourney gives the most artistic control and the most stylistically coherent outputs — if you have access, it's the strongest choice for this kind of set. DALL-E inside ChatGPT is the easiest entry: no extra account, no waiting list. Adobe Firefly integrates directly with Canva and Photoshop, so if you're already working there, it saves a step. Canva AI keeps everything inside one workspace. Stable Diffusion is free and runs locally, but has the steepest setup cost. The decision rule is this: pick the one you already have access to and do not trial three tools. The consistency check only works if all three images come from the same tool with the same seed settings. Switching tools mid-set breaks the visual grammar even if the skeleton is identical. The tool is not the assignment — the three images are.

props.artifactTitle: Pick one tool — the consistency rule
props.artifactHeading: Pick one tool — the consistency rule
artifactLines: Midjourney — most artistic control; best for stylistically coherent sets.
DALL-E in ChatGPT — easiest access; no extra account or waitlist.
Adobe Firefly — integrates with Canva and Photoshop; skip an export step.
Canva AI — stays in one workspace; good if you're already building there.
Stable Diffusion — free and local; steepest setup cost.
Rule: pick one. All three images must come from the same tool in the same session.
props.sparkLine: Switching tools mid-set breaks consistency even with the same skeleton.

beats.act: generate 3 and run the consistency check
beats.narration_text: After you have all three images, run the consistency check before you touch PDF or Canva. Place the three images side by side — in a browser tab, in a folder preview, or in Canva — and ask three yes-or-no questions. Question one: are they in the same color family? If the palette words were locked, the answer should be yes. Question two: are they in the same visual style? If the style tag was locked, the answer should be yes. Question three: do they have the same level of detail and rendering quality? If the lighting or medium was locked, the answer should be yes. The rule is this: if two of the three answers are no, the skeleton is broken. Do not try to fix it with post-processing. Go back, revise the style tag or the palette words, and regenerate all three. You are regenerating because the constraint system failed, not because one image looked bad.

shot.new_visual_element: Three-image consistency checker — side-by-side comparison with three yes/no criteria overlaid: (1) Same color family? (2) Same visual style? (3) Same level of detail? Pass/fail indicator per criterion. If 2 of 3 fail — regenerate all three.

props.failRule: If 2 of 3 answers are No — revise skeleton and regenerate all three.

beats.narration_text: When all three images pass the consistency check, assemble them into a PDF. Download all three images from the tool. Open Canva, Google Slides, or any PDF-capable application. Create three pages — one image per page. Use full bleed or centered layout — whichever you choose, apply it consistently to all three pages. Do not mix full bleed on page one and centered on page two. Export as PDF. That PDF is the deliverable. The submission is three images that read as a set, exported as a single document. It is not a mood board, a collage, or a presentation with titles and bullets. One image per page, three pages, one PDF.

artifactLines: Step 1: Download all three images from the generation tool.
Step 2: Open Canva, Google Slides, or any PDF-capable app. One image per page — consistent layout (all full-bleed or all centered).
Step 3: Export as PDF. That is the deliverable.
Not a mood board. Not a collage. One image per page, three pages, one PDF.
props.sparkLine: Consistent layout across all three pages — not one choice per page.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Episode 5. One skeleton prompt, three subject swaps. The style tag, the palette words, and the lighting or medium are locked — they never change across the three images. The subject is the only variable. All three images come from the same tool in the same session. You run the consistency check — same color family, same style, same detail level — before you open a PDF tool. If two of three checks fail, you regenerate all three from a revised skeleton. Images that look like three different art styles, or three different color worlds, do not constitute a set — they constitute three separate images. The images must also say something about the person or brand, not just look good in isolation.

props.artifactTitle: 3 On-Brand AI Images That Look Like a Set
artifactLines: One skeleton prompt: style tag + palette words + lighting locked; subject swapped three times.
Same tool for all three — same session. Tool-switching breaks consistency.
Consistency check before PDF: same color family, same style, same detail level.
If 2 of 3 checks fail — revise the skeleton and regenerate all three.
Images must say something about the person or brand, not just look good independently.

beats.narration_text: Your Turn. Open your brand sheet and pull out three things: your style tag, your palette words, and your lighting or medium preference. If you don't have those defined yet, decide them now — one word or phrase each — before you open any image tool. Then paste this skeleton three times into your chosen tool, changing only the subject line for each of the three images. Run the consistency check when all three are generated. Place them side by side, answer the three yes-or-no questions, and only move to PDF assembly when you have three yes answers. If something fails, go back to the skeleton, revise the locked variable that broke, and regenerate all three from that revised version. The PDF with all three images is what you submit.

props.segment: 3 On-Brand AI Images That Look Like a Set
props.command: [STYLE_TAG — LOCK], [PALETTE_WORDS — LOCK], [LIGHTING/MEDIUM — LOCK], representing [SUBJECT — SWAP THIS ONCE PER IMAGE]. IMAGE 1 SUBJECT: my expertise area — [EXPERTISE]. IMAGE 2 SUBJECT: my industry or field — [INDUSTRY]. IMAGE 3 SUBJECT: my core value or offering — [VALUE]. Paste this skeleton three times. Replace only the SUBJECT line. Use the same tool for all three.
props.runningText: paste into your chosen image tool — swap the SUBJECT line three times…
output: All three generated? Run the consistency check before opening Canva.
Same color family · Same style · Same detail level — three yes answers.
Then: one image per page, three pages, export PDF.

beats.narration_text: 3 On-Brand AI Images That Look Like a Set. Liam, in for Bear.
